worth_it_explanation: Child-directed treatment decides whether the ad serving on kids programming suppresses personalized advertising and the data uses that go with it, which is where the COPPA liability sits for a video publisher. The check is a review of how inventory likely to reach children is flagged inside FreeWheel and whether the suppression follows from that flag, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time. The finding points to a concrete configuration the publisher can correct before a regulator or a demand partner finds the gap.
intro: A video and Connected TV (CTV) publisher running kids programming through FreeWheel carries a duty under the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) to keep personalized advertising and certain data uses off inventory directed to children under 13. The ad server has to know which inventory is child-directed before it can suppress the wrong demand, so the flag on that inventory is the control that everything else depends on. This subcategory checks whether the child-directed inventory is identified inside FreeWheel and whether the suppression of personalized ads and restricted data processing actually follows from that identification, rather than being assumed.

audit_checks.title: Child-directed inventory is not flagged for personalized-ad suppression
audit_checks.impact_rationale: If kids programming is not flagged as child-directed inside FreeWheel, the ad server treats it like any other inventory and lets personalized demand and the data collection behind it run against an audience the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) protects. That is a direct compliance exposure with regulatory and contractual consequences, not a yield tuning question, which puts it above the routine configuration checks.

educate.base:
COPPA governs the collection and use of personal information from children under 13, and the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) treats persistent identifiers used to serve targeted advertising as personal information under the rule. For a video or Connected TV (CTV) publisher, that means personalized advertising on inventory directed to children cannot run the way it runs on general inventory because the identifiers and behavioral signals that power personalized demand are exactly what the rule restricts.

COPPA does not only reach sites and services aimed solely at kids. The FTC's guidance is explicit that a general-audience service falls under the rule once it has actual knowledge it is collecting information from a child under 13, and that mixed-audience content directed in part to children is treated as child-directed for the audience that is under 13. A broadcaster or streaming publisher with a children's channel, a kids section, or individual kids titles inside a wider catalog is squarely in scope for that inventory.

FreeWheel serves ads against the inventory the publisher describes to it, so the ad server cannot apply child-directed treatment to inventory it has not been told is child-directed. The flag on that inventory is the control. When kids programming is marked as child-directed, the publisher can suppress personalized demand and switch the ad serving to contextual, non-personalized advertising for those impressions. When the flag is missing, the ad server has no reason to treat the inventory differently, so personalized demand and the data collection behind it run against the protected audience.

A few signs point to child-directed inventory that is not flagged:
  • Kids channels, sections, or titles are served through the same FreeWheel configuration as general inventory with no child-directed designation recorded
  • Personalized or behaviorally targeted demand appears in reporting against inventory the publisher knows reaches children under 13
  • No mapping exists between the inventory a publisher would describe as kids programming and a child-directed setting inside the ad server
You may want to confirm that every piece of inventory reaching children under 13 carries a child-directed designation that drives non-personalized serving. One approach is to list the kids channels, sections, and titles, then trace each to the FreeWheel setting that suppresses personalized demand for it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster service, kids programming can sit as a dedicated channel, a section of a larger app, or individual titles scattered through a general catalog. You may want to map each of those forms to its FreeWheel configuration because a title-level kids show inside a general-audience app is the case most likely to miss a child-directed flag that a whole dedicated channel would catch.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video service, product or how-to videos aimed at families or children carry the same exposure as editorial kids content since the audience under 13 is what COPPA protects regardless of the commercial intent. You may want to flag family-facing and child-facing commerce video as child-directed so personalized demand is suppressed on it the same way it is on editorial kids inventory.

learn_more.source_title: Complying with COPPA: Frequently Asked Questions
Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule: Not Just for Kids' Sites

investigate.intro: Review which inventory reaches children under 13 and whether each piece of it carries a child-directed designation inside FreeWheel that suppresses personalized demand.

freewheel_console.label: FreeWheel console
steps: List the channels, sections, or titles the publisher considers child-directed or likely to reach children under 13.
For each, open its configuration in FreeWheel and record whether a child-directed designation is set and whether it switches the inventory to non-personalized serving.
Note any kids inventory served through the same configuration as general inventory with no child-directed setting recorded.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory the publisher identified as child-directed, showing the demand types that served against it.
Look for personalized or behaviorally targeted demand running against inventory that reaches children under 13.
Record any inventory where personalized demand served and no child-directed flag was set, which is the gap to correct.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per piece of inventory that reaches children under 13, whether a child-directed designation is set in FreeWheel and whether personalized demand is suppressed for it. Inventory where every child-directed piece carries the designation and serves only non-personalized demand passes. Inventory the publisher knows reaches children that runs through a general configuration with no child-directed flag, or that shows personalized demand in reporting, is a fail recorded with the channels or titles affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the child-directed designation and the demand types served for each piece of kids inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} serves inventory reaching children under 13 without a child-directed designation in FreeWheel, so personalized demand and the data collection behind it run against an audience the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) protects. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) treats persistent identifiers used for targeted advertising as personal information under the rule, and the ad server cannot suppress personalized demand on inventory it has not been told is child-directed. {{voice_recommend}} flagging every kids channel, section, and title as child-directed in FreeWheel so those impressions serve contextual, non-personalized advertising, then confirming in reporting that personalized demand no longer appears against that inventory.

audit_checks.title: Restricted data processing is not applied to inventory likely to reach minors
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Flagging child-directed inventory suppresses personalized demand, but the privacy signal passed downstream and the data uses on mixed-audience inventory still have to match. When restricted data processing is not applied to inventory likely to reach minors, demand partners receive signals that permit data uses the publisher should be limiting, which is a real exposure on the edges of the catalog rather than the catastrophic gap of unflagged core kids inventory.

educate.base:
Flagging an inventory as child-directed is the first control, but it is not the whole picture. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) restricts how a child's personal information is used and disclosed, not only whether an ad is personalized, so the data processing tied to those impressions has to be limited as well. The privacy signal a publisher passes to its demand partners is what tells the downstream chain that the impression is child-directed and that data uses on it are restricted.

The harder cases sit on the edges of the catalog. Inventory that is not a dedicated kids channel but is still likely to reach children, such as family programming, a general-audience title with a strong under-13 following, or a mixed-audience section, needs the same restricted treatment for the portion of the audience that is under 13. The Federal Trade Commission's (FTC) six-step compliance guidance treats data collection and disclosure restrictions as a core obligation alongside notice and consent, which means the data side of the treatment matters as much as the ad-personalization side.

FreeWheel and the wider video supply chain carry privacy and consent signals through standards-based mechanisms. The Global Privacy Platform (GPP) from the IAB Tech Lab is the protocol that transmits privacy, consent, and consumer-choice signals from a publisher's app or site through to demand partners. For child-directed and minor-likely inventory, the signal the publisher sets has to communicate the restriction, so that partners downstream apply the limited data processing the inventory requires rather than treating the impression as fully open.

A few signs point to restricted data processing that is not applied:
  • Child-directed inventory is flagged for non-personalized ads but the privacy signal passed to demand partners does not communicate the restriction
  • Family or mixed-audience inventory likely to reach children under 13 is treated as general inventory for data-processing purposes
  • No record exists of which inventory carries a restricted-data signal and what that signal communicates downstream
You may want to confirm that the privacy signal on child-directed and minor-likely inventory communicates the data restriction, not only the ad-personalization suppression. One approach is to inspect the signal passed for a sample of kids and family impressions and confirm it limits the data uses COPPA restricts.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcaster service, family programming and general-audience titles with a young following sit between dedicated kids channels and general inventory, so they are the inventory most likely to miss restricted data processing. You may want to decide a treatment for that middle band and apply the restricted-data signal to it rather than defaulting it to general handling.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video service, family-facing product and instructional videos can reach children under 13 even when the commercial intent is aimed at a parent. You may want to apply restricted data processing to that family-facing inventory so the privacy signal passed downstream limits data uses on the under-13 portion of the audience.

learn_more.source_title: Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule: A Six-Step Compliance Plan for Your Business

investigate.intro: Review whether the privacy signal passed for child-directed and minor-likely inventory communicates a data-processing restriction, and whether mixed-audience inventory is included.

freewheel_console.label: FreeWheel console
steps: List the inventory flagged as child-directed plus any family or mixed-audience inventory likely to reach children under 13.
For each, record what privacy and consent signal FreeWheel is configured to pass downstream for those impressions.
Note any child-directed or minor-likely inventory where the signal does not communicate a data-processing restriction.

signal_inspection.label: Privacy signal inspection
steps: Capture the privacy signal passed for a sample of kids, family, and general impressions using the player or a network-request capture, e.g., the Global Privacy Platform (GPP) string sent with the ad request.
Compare the signal on child-directed and minor-likely impressions against the signal on general impressions to confirm the restriction is communicated.
Record any case where minor-likely inventory carries the same open signal as general inventory.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per piece of child-directed and minor-likely inventory, whether the privacy signal passed downstream communicates a data-processing restriction. Inventory where the signal on every child-directed and minor-likely piece limits the restricted data uses passes. Inventory likely to reach children under 13 that passes the same open signal as general inventory is a fail recorded with the inventory and the signal observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the privacy signal passed for each piece of child-directed and minor-likely inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} suppresses personalized ads on child-directed inventory but does not apply restricted data processing to inventory likely to reach minors, so the privacy signal passed to demand partners permits data uses the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) restricts. Family and mixed-audience video sits between dedicated kids channels and general inventory, and the data side of the treatment matters as much as ad-personalization suppression. {{voice_recommend}} setting the privacy signal on child-directed and minor-likely inventory so it communicates the data restriction downstream, for example through the Global Privacy Platform (GPP) string FreeWheel passes, then confirming a sampled signal on kids and family impressions limits the restricted data uses.
